_purpose: Focused validation canaries for the newly-wisdom'd and newly-vendored web libraries: Chart.js, GSAP, canvas-confetti, pixi.js (v7), matter.js, globe.gl (+ bundled world-countries GeoJSON), and anime.js. A prompt or two per library, chosen to exercise each lib's flagship capability and to RETRY the genres that previously failed (pixi sprite-heavy + shader filters, matter physics boards). Each library is auto-vendored offline when an artifact references it via a normal versioned <script src> CDN tag (the WEBLIB B-catch rewrites it to the local ./libs vendored copy). 'lane' is the vendored library id (chart/gsap/confetti/pixi/matter/globe/anime). Every goal demands a complete interactive result on first load, full offline operation, and mobile-friendly touch + responsive layout. Run via runWizard.

entries.goal: Build a sleek live metrics dashboard using Chart.js: a responsive grid of cards each holding a beautiful animated chart - a streaming line chart that scrolls in new points every tick, an animated doughnut, a live-updating bar chart, and a radar - all fed by simulated inline data generators (), in a dark dashboard theme with neon accent strokes and soft glows. Set responsive:true and maintainAspectRatio:false on every chart so each fills its card and reflows; push points and call chart.update('none') on an interval for smooth streaming. A pause/resume button stops and restarts the feeds. Must run , present a complete live dashboard on first load, and be mobile-friendly: the grid collapses to one column on narrow screens and works with touch. Use the REAL library via a normal <script src> CDN tag (the platform vendors it to a local offline copy automatically -- do NOT hand-roll or reimplement the library).

Build an animated bar-chart race using Chart.js: a horizontal bar chart where category bars grow, reorder, and overtake each other smoothly as a simulated timeline advances year by year, with a big animated year label and value tickers, in a vibrant categorical palette with crisp typography on a clean light card. Drive it with inline sample data per year (); on each step update chart.data and call chart.update() so Chart.js tweens the bars, and sort the dataset each frame so the leader rises to the top. Play/pause and a speed control. Must run , show a complete running race on first load, and be mobile-friendly: the chart uses responsive:true with maintainAspectRatio:false to fill its container and reflow, and works with touch. Use the REAL library via a normal <script src> CDN tag (the platform vendors it to a local offline copy automatically -- do NOT hand-roll or reimplement the library).

Build a cinematic animated hero showpiece using GSAP: a single orchestrated gsap.timeline() where a logo assembles from scattered shapes, a headline reveals word by word with a stagger, accent underlines draw on, and floating background shapes drift in parallax, in a bold modern palette of deep navy, coral, and cream. Use gsap.timeline() with chained .to/.from/.fromTo tweens and eases (power2/power4/back) plus stagger for the words; a Replay button calls timeline.restart(). Wrap the autoplay in a prefers-reduced-motion check (matchMedia) and jump to the end state instead of animating when reduced motion is on. Must run , present the finished composition (animated, then settled) on first load, and be mobile-friendly: fully responsive layout that fills the viewport and works on touch. Use the REAL library via a normal <script src> CDN tag (the platform vendors it to a local offline copy automatically -- do NOT hand-roll or reimplement the library).

Build a soothing SVG morph showpiece using GSAP: a single luminous SVG blob that smoothly morphs between several organic shapes on an endless eased loop, with a slow color-gradient drift through teal, violet, and rose and a soft drop shadow. Drive the morph by tweening the path 'd' attribute between shapes that share the same point count (gsap.to(path, {attr:{d: nextD}, duration, ease:'sine.inOut'}) in a repeating timeline with yoyo or a sequence), and animate an SVG <linearGradient> stops for the color drift. Tapping nudges the morph to the next shape early. Respect prefers-reduced-motion (matchMedia) by holding a static shape. Must run , present a live morphing blob on first load, and be mobile-friendly: responsive viewBox-scaled SVG that fills the viewport and works on touch. Use the REAL library via a normal <script src> CDN tag (the platform vendors it to a local offline copy automatically -- do NOT hand-roll or reimplement the library).

Build a delightful celebration toy using canvas-confetti: a big friendly 'Celebrate!' button that fires a gorgeous confetti burst, plus extra buttons for distinct styles (a fireworks volley of staggered bursts, dual side cannons firing inward, a gentle confetti rain from the top, and a golden shower), and a click-or-tap-anywhere mode that pops confetti from the exact pointer position by mapping the event coordinates into confetti's origin {x,y} in 0..1 space, all over a warm festive animated gradient backdrop. Vary particleCount, spread, angle, startVelocity, and colors per style so each feels different and tasteful. Must run , be immediately satisfying and replayable on first load, and be mobile-friendly: large tappable buttons, responsive layout that fills the viewport, and touch triggers the same bursts as mouse. Use the REAL library via a normal <script src> CDN tag (the platform vendors it to a local offline copy automatically -- do NOT hand-roll or reimplement the library).

Build a vibrant bullet-hell arcade shooter using pixi.js (v7) that comfortably renders around ten thousand glowing bullets at 60fps: your ship weaves through gorgeous dense spiral and ring patterns fired by rotating enemies, with score, lives, waves, and a slow-motion focus mode, in a neon palette of hot pink, cyan, and gold on black. Push every bullet sprite into a PIXI.ParticleContainer (created with maxSize and the position/rotation/alpha property flags) and advance them in a single app.ticker loop using delta for frame-rate independence; recycle bullets from a fixed pool instead of allocating each frame. Drag or arrow keys move the ship; hold to focus-fire and slow time. Must run , be fully playable on first load, and be mobile-friendly: the stage resizes to the viewport (renderer.resize on window resize) and drag-to-move works on touch. Use the REAL library via a normal <script src> CDN tag (the platform vendors it to a local offline copy automatically -- do NOT hand-roll or reimplement the library).

Build a filter-driven shader kaleidoscope using pixi.js (v7): a swirling generative texture pushed through pixi filters - a PIXI.DisplacementFilter driven by a scrolling displacement sprite plus a ColorMatrixFilter hue rotation - then mirrored into radial symmetry, blooming into an ever-changing mandala in iridescent jewel tones. Render a moving source container to a RenderTexture, then draw it rotated/reflected into N wedges each frame in the app.ticker; moving the pointer warps the displacement scale and offset live, and a slider sets the symmetry count. Keep it smooth by reusing one RenderTexture and updating filter uniforms rather than rebuilding objects. Must run , present a live hypnotic kaleidoscope on first load, and be mobile-friendly: the stage resizes to the viewport and pointer warping works with touch drag. Use the REAL library via a normal <script src> CDN tag (the platform vendors it to a local offline copy automatically -- do NOT hand-roll or reimplement the library).

Build a satisfying neon Plinko board using matter.js: drop glowing pucks from the top so they bounce through a staggered triangular peg field into scoring slots at the bottom, with a running score, a balls-remaining counter, a 'Drop' button, and tap-anywhere-along-the-top to drop a puck at that x, in a vibrant arcade palette of electric pink, cyan, and gold on dark. Build the pegs as static circle bodies in a Matter.Composite, the slot dividers and floor as static rectangles, and the pucks as restitution-tuned circles added to the Matter.Engine; run the world with Matter.Runner and render with Matter.Render (or a custom canvas) sized to the viewport. Detect which slot a puck lands in via a collision/region check and add its points. Must run with real physics bounces, present a ready board on first load, and be mobile-friendly: the canvas resizes to the viewport and tap-to-drop works on touch. Use the REAL library via a normal <script src> CDN tag (the platform vendors it to a local offline copy automatically -- do NOT hand-roll or reimplement the library).

Build a domino-topple toy using matter.js: tap or drag to lay a winding row of upright dominoes along a path, then press 'Go' to tip the first one and watch a satisfying chain reaction topple the whole line, in a clean bright palette of bold colored dominoes on a pale floor. Each domino is a tall thin rectangle body with sensible density and friction added to the Matter.Engine world (with a static ground); start the chain by applying a small angular impulse or force to the first body, and a counter tracks how many have fallen by checking each body's angle past a threshold. A reset button clears and rebuilds the board. Run the world via Matter.Runner and a canvas renderer sized to the viewport. Must run with real toppling physics, present an interactive board on first load, and be mobile-friendly: the canvas resizes to the viewport and placing dominoes works on touch. Use the REAL library via a normal <script src> CDN tag (the platform vendors it to a local offline copy automatically -- do NOT hand-roll or reimplement the library).

Build a spinning 3D data globe with glowing arcs between world capitals using globe.gl: a smoothly auto-rotating colored Earth with luminous animated dashed arcs connecting capital cities, hover tooltips on each arc, and a short title/legend, in a cosmic palette of deep space backdrop, a teal-blue globe surface, and warm gold-to-cyan arcs. Load globe.gl via a single UMD <script src> (it bundles three.js, so do NOT also load three). CRITICAL for : do NOT set any external globeImageUrl/bumpImageUrl/backgroundImageUrl - render a solid colored globe with .globeMaterial(new THREE.MeshPhongMaterial({color, shininess:8})), .showAtmosphere(true).atmosphereColor(...).atmosphereAltitude(0.18), and a color .backgroundColor(...). Define capital lat/lng and the arc pairs INLINE in code (no fetch); for country outlines use the bundled GeoJSON via <script src='./libs/world-countries.js'></script> which sets window.WORLD_COUNTRIES. Wire arcsData with arcDashLength/arcDashGap/arcDashAnimateTime for the flowing look and enable controls().autoRotate. Must run (no external images or data), present a complete spinning globe with visible arcs on first load, and be mobile-friendly: size the container to the viewport and update width/height on resize - globe.gl OrbitControls already give one-finger rotate and two-finger zoom on touch. Use the REAL library via a normal <script src> CDN tag (the platform vendors it to a local offline copy automatically -- do NOT hand-roll or reimplement the library).

Build a population-spikes 3D globe using globe.gl: a slowly auto-rotating colored Earth studded with glowing vertical spikes rising from major cities, each spike's height and color mapped to that city's population, with hover tooltips showing the city name and value, in a dark-space palette with a deep-indigo globe surface and an accent gradient from cool to hot on the tallest spikes. Load globe.gl via a single UMD <script src> (it bundles three.js, so do NOT also load three or a three importmap). CRITICAL for : do NOT set any external globeImageUrl/bumpImageUrl/backgroundImageUrl - render a solid colored globe via .globeMaterial(new THREE.MeshPhongMaterial({color, shininess:8})), .showAtmosphere(true).atmosphereColor(...).atmosphereAltitude(0.18), and a color .backgroundColor(...). Define the city lat/lng/population data INLINE in code (a few dozen cities, no fetch) and use pointsData with pointLat/pointLng, pointAltitude mapped from population, pointColor from a value ramp, and pointRadius; optionally load the bundled ./libs/world-countries.js (window.WORLD_COUNTRIES) for faint country outlines. Enable controls().autoRotate and set an initial pointOfView. Must run , present a complete spiked globe on first load, and be mobile-friendly: container sized to the viewport, width/height updated on resize, and built-in OrbitControls handle touch rotate and pinch zoom. Use the REAL library via a normal <script src> CDN tag (the platform vendors it to a local offline copy automatically -- do NOT hand-roll or reimplement the library).

Build a delightful staggered-reveal motion piece using anime.js: a grid (or radial cluster) of tiles that cascade into view with a beautifully eased staggered animation - selectable wave, spiral, and random orders via buttons - each tile a cell of a gradient mosaic in sunset oranges, pinks, and purples. Use anime() with the targets selector and anime.stagger() for the delay (including grid-based and from:'center' staggering for the wave/spiral patterns), animating translateY/scale/opacity with a springy or cubic ease; a Replay button re-runs the chosen pattern by restarting the anime instance, and tapping a tile fires a quick bounce pop on just that element. Wrap autoplay in a prefers-reduced-motion (matchMedia) check that snaps tiles to their final state instead of animating. Must run , present the completed reveal (animated, then settled) on first load, and be mobile-friendly: responsive grid that fills the viewport and pops tiles on touch. Use the REAL library via a normal <script src> CDN tag (the platform vendors it to a local offline copy automatically -- do NOT hand-roll or reimplement the library).
